content.text: Control Owner Audit Preparation Playbooks

Audit preparation fails when it is treated as a compliance team responsibility rather than a control owner responsibility. The compliance team coordinates and reviews; the control owners gather, verify, and present their own evidence. A control owner who does not know what their control does, who approved it, or when it was last tested will lose credibility with auditors instantly.

This sub-subtopic provides role-specific preparation playbooks for the control owners most involved in Claude governance: the Platform Engineering Lead (technical controls), the DPO (data controls), the MRM/Compliance Lead (model risk controls), and the Business Owner (operational controls).
The −12 Week Evidence Gap Review
Twelve weeks before the audit starts, each control owner must run a gap review against their assigned controls. The goal is to surface evidence gaps while there is enough time to fix them.
Gap Review Checklist

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date
import json

@dataclass
class EvidenceGap:
    control_id: str
    gap_description: str
    severity: str          # "blocker" | "significant" | "minor"
    remediation: str
    owner: str
    due_date: str          # ISO date — must be before T-4 weeks

@dataclass
class GapReview:
    reviewer: str
    review_date: str
    audit_period_start: str
    audit_period_end: str
    gaps: list[EvidenceGap] = field(default_factory=list)

    def has_blockers(self) -> bool:
        return any(g.severity == "blocker" for g in self.gaps)

    def summary(self) -> str:
        total = len(self.gaps)
        blockers = sum(1 for g in self.gaps if g.severity == "blocker")
        significant = sum(1 for g in self.gaps if g.severity == "significant")
        return (
            f"Gap review by {self.reviewer} for {self.audit_period_start}–{self.audit_period_end}: "
            f"{total} gaps ({blockers} blockers, {significant} significant)"
        )

# Platform Engineering Lead gap review
review = GapReview(
    reviewer="AI Platform Lead",
    review_date="2026-04-01",
    audit_period_start="2026-01-01",
    audit_period_end="2026-03-31",
)

# Check evidence dir for each control
evidence_dir = Path("evidence")
control_checks = {
    "CONS-001": "api_logs",
    "PII-001":  "api_logs",
    "OUT-001":  "api_logs",
    "TOK-001":  "api_logs",
    "SPH-001":  "validation",
}
for control_id, subdir in control_checks.items():
    path = evidence_dir / subdir
    files = list(path.glob(f"{control_id}_*.json")) if path.exists() else []
    if not files:
        review.gaps.append(EvidenceGap(
            control_id=control_id,
            gap_description=f"No evidence files found for {control_id} in period",
            severity="blocker",
            remediation="Check logging pipeline; verify middleware is writing to correct directory",
            owner="AI Platform Lead",
            due_date="2026-05-15",
        ))

print(review.summary())
for g in review.gaps:
    print(f"  [{g.severity.upper()}] {g.control_id}: {g.gap_description}")
Platform Engineering Lead Playbook

The Platform Engineering Lead owns the technical controls: consent gate (CONS-001), PII redaction (PII-001), output redaction (OUT-001), token anomaly detection (TOK-001), and system prompt integrity (SPH-001).
Pre-Audit Preparation Steps
Run the nightly evidence aggregator for the full audit period to confirm all days have daily summary records. Identify any gaps and retrieve the raw log files as a fallback.
Verify the git history of the system prompt template to confirm no unauthorised changes were made during the audit period. Export the git log as a signed PDF for the evidence package.
Run the full automated test suite (consent gate, PII redaction, output redaction, sensitivity tests) and capture the output as a timestamped JSON file. This is the 'operating at audit time' evidence.
Confirm that model version pins in the production configuration match the anthropic_model_id fields in the MRM inventory. Export a screenshot or configuration file as evidence.
Prepare a one-page architecture diagram showing the data flow through all five controls. Auditors use this to frame their testing scope.
Likely Auditor Questions and Answers
Question
Expected Answer
Supporting Evidence
How do you know the consent gate operated correctly for every call in Q1?
The consent_check field in every APICallLog record captures the gate decision. The daily summary aggregates show consent denial count for each day.
Daily summary files CONS-001_2026-01-01.json through 2026-03-31.json; raw logs for sample days
What would happen if the consent check service was unavailable?
The gated_claude_call() function raises PermissionError if the consent check returns an error — it fails closed, not open.
Unit test: test_consent_gate_fails_closed.json; code review of controls.py
Who can modify the system prompt in production?
System prompts are version-controlled in git. Production reads from a specific git tag; changes require a PR review by the AI Safety Lead and a deploy pipeline run.
Git tag history; deploy pipeline configuration; PR review log
DPO / Privacy Lead Playbook
The DPO owns data-related controls: consent audit trail (ACT-001), PII detection and redaction (PII-001), and the DPIA/RoPA entries for Claude deployments.
Pre-Audit Preparation Steps

Export the consent audit trail for the audit period and verify it is complete — every API call with a 'denied' consent decision should have a corresponding audit record showing when consent was last valid for that user.
Pull the PII sampling report for each month of the audit period and confirm that the sampling rate (minimum 5%) was maintained and that the false-negative rate is below the documented threshold.
Verify the DPIA for each Claude deployment is current (reviewed within 12 months) and that the RoPA entries reflect the actual data flows as implemented, not just the original design.
Confirm the data retention schedule has been enforced: API logs older than the retention period should have been archived or deleted. Export a deletion log or archival manifest as evidence.
Review any consent withdrawal requests received during the audit period and confirm each was processed within the committed SLA (typically 30 days for GDPR).
Data Subject Rights Response Log

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta
from pathlib import Path
import json

@dataclass
class DSRRequest:
    request_id: str
    request_type: str    # "access" | "erasure" | "restriction" | "portability"
    received_date: str   # ISO date
    user_id_hash: str    # pseudonymised
    claude_models_affected: list[str]
    completed_date: str = ""
    sla_days: int = 30

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        deadline = date.fromisoformat(self.received_date) + timedelta(days=self.sla_days)
        return not self.completed_date and date.today() > deadline

    def days_to_sla(self) -> int:
        deadline = date.fromisoformat(self.received_date) + timedelta(days=self.sla_days)
        if self.completed_date:
            return (deadline - date.fromisoformat(self.completed_date)).days
        return (deadline - date.today()).days

DSR_LOG = Path("evidence/dsr_requests.jsonl")
DSR_LOG.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

def log_dsr(req: DSRRequest) -> None:
    import dataclasses
    record = dataclasses.asdict(req)
    record["overdue"] = req.is_overdue()
    record["days_to_sla"] = req.days_to_sla()
    with DSR_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

# Auditors will check DSR completion rate and SLA compliance
# Prepare a summary: total requests, completed on time, average completion days
MRM / Compliance Lead Playbook
The MRM / Compliance Lead owns model risk controls: model inventory (MRM-001), validation records (VAL-001), exception register, and the periodic MRC committee reporting trail.
Pre-Audit Preparation Steps
Export the full model inventory as of the audit period end date. Confirm that every Active model has a current validation report (issued within the required re-validation window for its tier).

Pull all validation reports issued during the audit period. Verify that the challenge/response record is included in each report and that all High-severity findings have a documented remediation plan with a target date.

Export the exception register filtered to the audit period. For each closed exception, confirm the re-test evidence is attached. For each open exception, confirm the compensating control is documented.

Export the MRC committee minutes for each meeting during the audit period. Confirm that the minutes show model risk was a standing agenda item and that the committee received the inventory and KPI reports.
Prepare a summary table of all material changes (model upgrades, scope expansions, new deployments) during the audit period and the validation actions taken for each.
Mock Audit: Dry Run at T−4 Weeks
A mock audit catches presentation gaps that document reviews miss. Assign an internal team member who was not involved in any of the controls to play the auditor role for 2 hours:
Request the evidence package:
The mock auditor requests the package for a randomly selected control. The control owner must retrieve and present it within 15 minutes — simulating the speed of a real audit request.
Interview simulation:
The mock auditor asks 5–10 questions from the prepared list. Any hesitation, inconsistency, or 'I'll have to check' answer is recorded as a gap.
Evidence challenge:
The mock auditor selects 3 specific API call logs at random from the evidence store and asks the control owner to explain each field. Unknown fields are gaps.
Edge case probing:
'What happened on March 3rd?' (pick a date at random). The control owner must demonstrate that evidence for that date exists and is complete within 5 minutes.
The mock audit output should be a written punch list, not verbal feedback. Written gaps become a tracked remediation list; verbal feedback is forgotten by the next day.
Business Owner Playbook

The Business Owner does not own the technical controls but is accountable for the overall risk posture of the Claude deployment. Auditors ask business owners about decision oversight, human-in-the-loop processes, and what happens when the model produces an incorrect output.
Pre-Audit Preparation Steps

Prepare a process narrative (one to two pages) describing how Claude outputs are used in each business decision. This answers the auditor's primary question: 'Who is responsible if the model is wrong?'

Pull the override log for the audit period: every instance where a human reviewer changed or rejected a Claude-generated output. The override rate is a key indicator of model reliability from the business perspective.

Confirm that the intended-use and prohibited-use statements in the MRM inventory match actual usage. If the deployment has expanded beyond its original scope, this is a material change that required re-validation.
Review the user complaint register for AI-related errors during the audit period. Confirm each complaint was triaged, investigated, and closed within the documented SLA.
Prepare a summary of the training completion rate for staff who use Claude-generated outputs in their decisions. This supports the TRAIN-002 control evidence.
Override Log Schema

from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import json

@dataclass
class ModelOutputOverride:
    override_id: str
    model_record_id: str         # MRM inventory ID
    reviewer: str                # name + role
    override_date: str           # ISO date
    override_reason: str         # "factual_error" | "scope_violation" | "quality" | "policy"
    original_output_tokens: int
    action_taken: str            # what the reviewer did instead of using the output
    feedback_category: str       # helps track systemic issues

OVERRIDE_LOG = Path("evidence/overrides.jsonl")
OVERRIDE_LOG.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

def log_override(override: ModelOutputOverride) -> None:
    record = asdict(override)
    record["logged_utc"] = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    with OVERRIDE_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")

def override_summary(period_start: str, period_end: str, model_record_id: str) -> dict:
    overrides = []
    if not OVERRIDE_LOG.exists():
        return {"error": "no override log found"}
    for line in OVERRIDE_LOG.read_text().splitlines():
        rec = json.loads(line)
        if (rec.get("model_record_id") == model_record_id
                and period_start <= rec.get("override_date", "") <= period_end):
            overrides.append(rec)

    by_reason = {}
    for o in overrides:
        r = o["override_reason"]
        by_reason[r] = by_reason.get(r, 0) + 1

    return {
        "period": {"start": period_start, "end": period_end},
        "model_record_id": model_record_id,
        "total_overrides": len(overrides),
        "by_reason": by_reason,
    }

# Auditors compare total_overrides / total_model_calls to assess override rate
# A rising override rate is a red flag; typically review if > 30%
Evidence Consistency Cross-Check

Before delivering the audit package, the compliance team must run a consistency cross-check to ensure that numbers reported across different evidence files do not contradict each other. Inconsistencies are a common source of auditor follow-up questions.
Cross-Check
What to Verify
Inconsistency Risk
Daily summary vs raw log count
Daily summary total_calls == count of raw log files with that date prefix
Log aggregation bug may produce understated totals
MRM inventory vs validation reports
Every model with status=active has a validation report issued after last_validation_date
Orphaned active models without a validation report
Exception register vs audit log
Every T3 alert in the API log has a corresponding exception ticket
Untracked critical alerts — major finding
TPRM assessment vs DPA dates
DPA effective date matches TPRM assessment date; vendor not added before DPA signed
Vendor used before contractual protections in place
Training completion vs user roster
LMS report covers all users listed in the API access control list
Staff with API access not in training population

from pathlib import Path
import json

def cross_check_evidence(
    daily_summary_dir: str,
    api_log_dir: str,
    target_date: str,
) -> list[str]:
    """
    Cross-check: daily summary total_calls must match raw log file count.
    Returns a list of discrepancy descriptions (empty = clean).
    """
    issues = []
    summary_path = Path(daily_summary_dir) / f"{target_date}.json"
    if not summary_path.exists():
        issues.append(f"Missing daily summary for {target_date}")
        return issues

    summary = json.loads(summary_path.read_text())
    reported_total = summary.get("total_calls", -1)

    raw_count = len(list(Path(api_log_dir).glob("*.json")))
    # In a real system, filter raw logs by date; simplified here
    if reported_total != raw_count:
        issues.append(
            f"{target_date}: daily summary reports {reported_total} calls "
            f"but {raw_count} raw log files found"
        )
    return issues

# Run for all dates in the audit period
from datetime import date, timedelta

start = date(2026, 1, 1)
end = date(2026, 3, 31)
all_issues = []
current = start
while current <= end:
    issues = cross_check_evidence(
        "evidence/daily_summaries",
        "evidence/api_logs",
        current.isoformat(),
    )
    all_issues.extend(issues)
    current += timedelta(days=1)

if all_issues:
    print(f"Cross-check found {len(all_issues)} discrepancy(ies):")
    for i in all_issues[:5]:
        print(f"  {i}")
else:
    print("Cross-check passed: all daily summaries consistent with raw logs")
Post-Audit Actions
Even after the audit concludes, control owners have obligations:
Management letter response:

For each finding in the auditor's management letter, the control owner must provide a written response within 30 days: accept or contest the finding, and if accepted, provide a remediation plan with a target completion date.
Finding remediation:
Track each finding in the exception register with the audit as the source. The compliance team monitors remediation progress and reports status to the audit committee at the next meeting.
Lessons learned:
Run a 30-minute post-audit debrief with all control owners. Document: what evidence was hardest to retrieve, what questions were unexpected, and what preparation steps would be added next time.
Playbook updates:
Update each role-specific playbook based on lessons learned. The playbook for the next audit cycle should incorporate questions that were asked this time but not anticipated.
Summary

Control owner audit preparation requires each owner to run an independent gap review at T−12 weeks, remediate blockers by T−4 weeks, complete a mock audit dry run, and deliver a pre-assembled evidence package at T−1 week. Role-specific playbooks — for Platform Engineering, Privacy, and MRM leads — ensure each owner knows precisely what evidence to prepare, what questions to expect, and what edge cases auditors probe. Consistent, rehearsed responses across all control owners prevent the credibility gaps that generate management letter findings.
